factual-accuracy.requirement: States HCI timing guidelines approximately 0.1s instantaneous, 1s preserves thought, beyond 10s loses attention
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses latency tiers (e.g., 15–80ms, 180–600ms, >2,500ms) but does not state the classic HCI timing guidelines of ~0.1s as instantaneous, ~1s preserving thought, and >10s losing attention. Those specific 0.1s/1s/10s thresholds are never mentioned or approximated.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States inline suggestions should target 150-300ms first render to preserve flow state
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives latency targets like “<200ms” for single-line autocomplete and “200–600ms” for multi-line suggestions, but it never states that inline suggestions should specifically target a 150–300ms first-render window to preserve flow state. The exact 150–300ms range and the “first render” framing are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States multi-line suggestions acceptable at 300-1000ms with boundaries and visual affordances
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives a specific latency range for multi-line suggestions (“200–600ms”) and separately sets an explanation latency target (“<1,000ms”). It also recommends showing proactive suggestions at workflow boundaries and using implicit signals/idle periods, which are boundaries and visual/UX affordance guidance tied to acceptable timing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States comment-to-code generation tolerable at 0.5-2 seconds with streaming or progress indicators
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends complex comment-to-code generation latency targets under 2,500ms and notes that 1.2–2.4s can be viable, but it does not state that 0.5–2 seconds is tolerable specifically when paired with streaming or progress indicators. Streaming is mentioned for Amazon Bedrock, yet not tied to a 0.5–2s tolerability threshold for comment-to-code.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends streaming, progress indicators, or on-demand mode when generation exceeds timing budgets
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends on-demand invocation in several contexts (e.g., mid-debugging and complex architectural decisions) and also discusses streaming as a latency-optimization feature for Amazon Bedrock, aligning with using streaming/on-demand modes when generation is slower.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends on-demand or delayed triggers for debugging and proactive acceptable for feature development
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends on-demand invocation for mid-debugging sessions and complex decisions, while endorsing proactive suggestions as acceptable for feature development contexts like workflow boundaries and boilerplate/feature additions.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States debugging has shorter edit-run cycles (approximately 1 minute) than feature development (approximately 3 minutes)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses debugging vs. feature development interruption costs and provides various latency and timing figures, but it never states that debugging has ~1-minute edit-run cycles while feature development has ~3-minute cycles. No approximate 1-minute vs 3-minute edit-run comparison appears anywhere in the text.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States less-experienced developers show higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than 10+ year developers
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that junior/less experienced developers have higher adoption and accept AI suggestions more frequently, while senior (10+ years) developers are more selective and have lower adoption.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares GitHub Copilot inline suggestions, Tabnine multi-line predictions, and CodeWhisperer comment-to-code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly discusses GitHub Copilot’s inline suggestions, Tabnine’s multi-line predictions, and Amazon CodeWhisperer/Amazon Q Developer’s comment-to-code generation, contrasting their architectures and timing/latency characteristics.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports acceptance rates 25-35% in production deployment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports GitHub Copilot acceptance rates as “27% to 30%” and also cites a “33% acceptance” case study, both of which fall within the 25–35% range for production deployment.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports Copilot 10-15% acceptance gain OR CodeWhisperer 50-60% faster OR JetBrains 40-50% acceptance increase
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports GitHub Copilot acceptance rates around 27–30% and mentions JetBrains acceptance rates around ~50%, but it does not state a Copilot 10–15% acceptance gain, a CodeWhisperer 50–60% faster metric, or a JetBrains 40–50% acceptance increase. None of the required gain/speed ranges are explicitly provided as increases or faster performance.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies trade-off between acceptance-rate optimization and code quality or retention metrics
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes a “fundamental trade-off” where proactive suggestions can raise efficiency/acceptance but also disrupt workflow and reduce code understanding/maintainability, and it recommends tracking metrics beyond acceptance rate such as churn and rework.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes conditional decision framework specifying when proactive versus on-demand presentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides an explicit proactive vs. on-demand decision framework in section 6.2, listing concrete conditions for proactive timing (workflow boundaries, idle periods, boilerplate/docs) versus on-demand invocation (mid-debugging, complex architecture, after declines), and further tailors this by seniority in section 6.3.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Extracts vendor-specific deployment techniques with measured outcomes (e.g., filter +50% acceptance)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes vendor-specific deployment techniques (e.g., Copilot’s HTTP/2 multiplexing/cancellation and global routing; Tabnine’s hybrid local+cloud “team of models”; JetBrains’ local CatBoost filter) and pairs them with measured outcomes such as “under 200ms” latency, “52% engagement/62% dismissal,” and “~50% acceptance rate” after filtering.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides experience-level differentiated strategies with specific recommendations per cohort
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides distinct, concrete strategy sections for junior (2–5 years) versus senior (10+ years) developers, including specific recommendations on proactive vs on-demand suggestions, explanation/confidence indicators, and control mechanisms tailored to each cohort.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies escalation paths when timing budgets exceeded (streaming, progress indicators, on-demand)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response specifies escalation paths when timing budgets are exceeded by recommending streaming/TTFT optimization, loading explanations asynchronously, and switching between proactive and on-demand delivery based on disruption risk (e.g., on-demand during debugging, proactive at workflow boundaries/idle periods).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes appropriate trust calibration from blind acceptance with UI implementation patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts calibrated trust (confidence scores, quality indicators, forcing functions, verification workflows) with risks of blind acceptance (automation bias, overreliance, “blind trust” leading to later code removal). It also proposes concrete UI patterns such as confidence indicators at multiple granularities, dashboards, control mechanisms, progressive disclosure, and on-demand vs proactive triggering tied to workflow boundaries.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Acknowledges evidence gaps and recommends instrumentation for uncertain areas
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly identifies multiple evidence gaps in a dedicated “Research Gaps” section and notes where data is thin or inconclusive (e.g., lack of latency targets for Tabnine/Q Developer, lack of controlled comparisons). It also recommends instrumentation/telemetry and metrics to track (e.g., beyond acceptance rate: time-to-task-completion, churn, flow disruption frequency) to measure uncertain areas.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends proactive suggestions as universally beneficial without acknowledging flow state disruption or interruption cost research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes that proactive suggestions can be disruptive (e.g., citing interruption recovery costs and noting mid-task interventions are dismissed 62% of the time) and recommends on-demand invocation during debugging and complex work. It explicitly frames proactive vs. on-demand as a trade-off and references interruption-cost research, so it does not present proactive suggestions as universally beneficial.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise timing terminology (latency thresholds, render time, flow state) with explicit millisecond or second units
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses precise timing terminology and includes explicit units, e.g., TTFT values of 15–80ms and 180–600ms, Copilot “under 200 milliseconds,” lifecycle overhead of ~2.5 seconds, and recommended thresholds like <2,500ms and <1,000ms.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes findings by tool (Copilot, Tabnine, CodeWhisperer) and cross-cuts by experience levels and task types
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes separate sections discussing GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, and Amazon CodeWhisperer/Q Developer, and it repeatedly cross-cuts the synthesis by experience level (junior vs senior) and by task type (e.g., debugging vs feature development) when discussing timing, acceptance, and trust.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective research synthesis tone without marketing language or anecdotal personal experience
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, evidence-synthesis style with citations, quantified findings, and design recommendations, and it does not include personal anecdotes. While it contains some mildly promotional phrasing (e.g., “actionable guidance,” “comprehensive”), it avoids overt marketing language overall and maintains an objective research tone.

presentation-quality.requirement: Concludes key sections with specific design recommendations derived from the research evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response ends with a dedicated “Practical Design Recommendations” section that provides concrete, evidence-linked recommendations (e.g., latency targets, proactive vs on-demand timing rules, seniority-based adaptations, and explanation design principles) explicitly derived from the cited research findings.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents comparisons as prose paragraphs rather than using tables, parallel sections, or labeled side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple tables (e.g., latency taxonomy, productivity by experience, latency targets) and clearly labeled sections with headings rather than only prose paragraphs for comparisons.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites academic research on programmer interruption costs or AI code completion from arXiv, ACM, IEEE, or ResearchGate
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites academic research on programmer interruption costs (e.g., Parnin & Rugaber’s “Resumption Strategies for Interrupted Programming Tasks”) and multiple arXiv papers relevant to AI code completion and trust (e.g., arXiv 2601.10253, 2602.08915, 2601.08045). These sources include ACM/CACM and arXiv links.

citation-quality.requirement: References JetBrains AI assistant deployment metrics or timing research from blog.jetbrains.com or official JetBrains publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response references JetBrains AI assistant deployment metrics and timing-related findings, including JetBrains blog.jetbrains.com content (e.g., “AI Code Completion: Less Is More”) and JetBrains publications/studies (e.g., ProAIDE study and HAX team telemetry study). These constitute official JetBrains publications and include deployment/usage metrics.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from github.blog, microsoft.com/research, or ACM publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Microsoft productivity studies from microsoft.com/research (e.g., AI and Productivity Report, Time-Warp study, Overreliance review, FAccT 2024 trust paper) and includes an ACM publication link (CACM “Taking Flight with Copilot”).

citation-quality.requirement: References established HCI timing thresholds (Nielsen, speedcurve.com, or equivalent authoritative HCI sources)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites established HCI/interruptions research with timing benchmarks, including Gloria Mark’s interruption recovery time and Parnin & Rugaber’s resumption-delay findings, which are authoritative sources for timing thresholds relevant to flow disruption.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes research or metrics from at least 2 of AWS CodeWhisperer, Google Research, Tabnine, or other code completion vendors
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes metrics and research from at least two relevant vendors: Tabnine (e.g., hybrid architecture and acceptance rate claims) and AWS CodeWhisperer/Amazon Q Developer (e.g., Bedrock TTFT improvements and Q Developer discussion), and it also cites Google Research on trust in code completion.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations in cited research rather than personal anecdotes or team experiences
citation-quality.explanation: The response consistently bases timing and latency recommendations on cited studies and reports (e.g., Copilot QCon latency target, ProAIDE proactive timing results, interruption-cost literature, and trust-calibration papers) rather than personal anecdotes or team experiences.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations solely in personal blogs, forums, or social media without citing academic papers or official vendor research
citation-quality.explanation: The response grounds its timing and latency recommendations in a wide range of sources including academic papers (e.g., Parnin & Rugaber), Microsoft Research reports, JetBrains publications, and AWS/InfoQ vendor materials, not solely blogs/forums/social media.
